title: OT Event Severity Distribution and Security Posture Dashboard
app: `Cisco Cyber Vision Splunk Add-On` (Splunkbase 5748) · `Nozomi Networks Universal Add-on` (Splunkbase 6905)
spl:
index=nozomi sourcetype="nozomi:alert"
| eval severity_label=case(risk>=8, "Critical", risk>=6, "High", risk>=4, "Medium", risk<4, "Low")
| bin _time span=1d
| stats count as events by severity_label, type_id, _time
| eventstats sum(events) as daily_total by _time
| eval pct_of_total=round(events*100/daily_total, 1)
| table _time, severity_label, type_id, events, pct_of_total
description: A high-level view of all OT security events by severity and category provides security posture at a glance. Trending event volumes over time shows whether security is improving (fewer Critical/High events) or degrading. Supports CISO reporting and board-level OT security metrics.
value: A high-level view of all OT security events by severity and category provides security posture at a glance. Trending event volumes over time shows whether security is improving (fewer Critical/High events) or degrading. Supports CISO reporting and board-level OT security metrics.
implementation: Both platforms provide event aggregation for security posture dashboards. Build executive dashboard showing daily event volumes, severity distribution, and trend lines. Track week-over-week and month-over-month changes. Alert on sudden spikes in Critical/High events.
visualization: Severity distribution pie chart; daily event volume stacked bar chart; trend line overlay; category breakdown table.
knownFalsePositives: Severity mix shifts when alert policies are re-tuned, new rule packs deploy, or sites onboard gradually.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Cisco Cyber Vision Splunk Add-On` (Splunkbase 5748) · `Nozomi Networks Universal Add-on` (Splunkbase 6905).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype=cisco:cybervision:event`, `sourcetype=cisco:cybervision:syslog` · Nozomi: `sourcetype=nozomi:alert`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Both platforms provide event aggregation for security posture dashboards. Build executive dashboard showing daily event volumes, severity distribution, and trend lines. Track week-over-week and month-over-month changes. Alert on sudden spikes in Critical/High events.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=nozomi sourcetype="nozomi:alert"
| eval severity_label=case(risk>=8, "Critical", risk>=6, "High", risk>=4, "Medium", risk<4, "Low")
| bin _time span=1d
| stats count as events by severity_label, type_id, _time
| eventstats sum(events) as daily_total by _time
| eval pct_of_total=round(events*100/daily_total, 1)
| table _time, severity_label, type_id, events, pct_of_total
```

Understanding this SPL

**OT Event Severity Distribution and Security Posture Dashboard** — A high-level view of all OT security events by severity and category provides security posture at a glance. Trending event volumes over time shows whether security is improving (fewer Critical/High events) or degrading. Supports CISO reporting and board-level OT security metrics.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=cisco:cybervision:event`, `sourcetype=cisco:cybervision:syslog` · Nozomi: `sourcetype=nozomi:alert`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Cisco Cyber Vision Splunk Add-On` (Splunkbase 5748) · `Nozomi Networks Universal Add-on` (Splunkbase 6905). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: nozomi; **sourcetype**: nozomi:alert. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=nozomi, sourcetype="nozomi:alert". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **severity_label** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Discretizes time or numeric ranges with `bin`/`bucket`.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by severity_label, type_id, _time** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• `eventstats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by _time** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **pct_of_total** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Pipeline stage (see **OT Event Severity Distribution and Security Posture Dashboard**): table _time, severity_label, type_id, events, pct_of_total

Step 3 — Validate
Run the search for a time range that should include both routine and change activity, then compare the same assets, flows, and alerts in the Cisco Cyber Vision UI and in Nozomi Guardian or CMC, matching whichever source the event came from. Reconcile severities, counts, and key fields in both tools.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Severity distribution pie chart; daily event volume stacked bar chart; trend line overlay; category breakdown table.
grandmaExplanation: We track how many critical and high events we get over time so our OT security posture and tuning show up in one trend.
